responses.response: This argument correctly identifies that redesigning admissions without removing early selection treats a symptom rather than the underlying gatekeeping mechanism. That's a valid observation — my improved-admissions plan doesn't solve the whole equity problem. But A10 treats this as disqualifying, whereas my position is different: I'm not claiming admissions reform is a comprehensive solution; I'm claiming it serves fairness within the selective track that currently exists and my family navigates. My role as a parent in this system is to push for fair access within the structure we use, not to defend whether the structure itself is adequate for the entire school system. A10 would move me only if evidence showed talent identification is structurally impossible under early selection, not merely harder. I don't have that evidence. So I acknowledge the limit A10 identifies — admissions reform is partial — but maintain my position.

This argument identifies a critical blind spot in my reasoning. I've been assuming schools themselves remain stable while selective places shrink, so 'advanced programmes locally' becomes a real alternative. But A19 correctly shows that as state selective places tighten, wealthy families exit to private schools rather than accept a shrinking pool. Sorting migrates rather than disappears. By the time my younger child faces selection, I'm not facing just fewer places; I'm facing a deteriorated public-school system as middle-class families have left, and the 'advanced programme' I was counting on doesn't adequately serve families without private-school access. This mechanism directly undermines my unstated assumption that the school set can be reformed while staying stable.
responses.new_condition: Statutory caps or transparency mechanisms on private-school growth, so that private schools cannot absorb middle-class exodus and leave public comprehensives stratified by family resources. Without this constraint, S2 reproduces sorting rather than reducing it.

responses.response: This argument correctly shows that comprehensive systems work empirically in Nordic countries, so my concern is not really about the structure itself. But that reveals my concern is implementation without the supporting infrastructure that made those systems work. A29 supplies the qualifier: Nordic track removal was bundled with universal teacher retraining, curriculum standardization, and regulated private alternatives — none in place in Hungary's scenario. So I'm not opposing the structure in principle; I'm opposing incomplete implementation where my child becomes a test case for an unproven local adaptation. A35 shows the structure can work, but only with the prerequisites bundled together.

This argument directly falsifies my stated condition. I said I would support S4 if compensation were placed on 'a multi-year, hard-to-cut budget footing,' but A41 correctly identifies that the scenario itself characterizes compensation as the first line item cut in fiscal consolidation. So I'm asking for entrenchment that system architecture makes impossible. This means S4 is not more stable than I claimed — it just delays the reckoning until the first budget squeeze. Families planning around compensation promises face greater risk than families with statutory structural reform, not less. This substantially revises my support.
responses.new_condition: S4 alone is not adequate. Only acceptable as a temporary measure explicitly bundled with legally entrenched structural reform (e.g., moving selection to age 14), not as a permanent non-structural solution. Without the structural change, compensation merely entrenches selection as permanent.
